data.short_description: Romance beat sheet for the Novel Studio app: the four-phase romance arc in the tradition of Gwen Hayes's Romancing the Beat, condensed to sixteen beats as an adoptable template. The beats live in this entity's lore; a novel adopts the sheet in its Blueprint, and its scenes' beat property uses these beat names exactly.

lore.title: Beats
lore.content:
Sixteen beats of the four-phase romance arc, in the tradition of Gwen Hayes's Romancing the Beat (condensed and adapted), in story order. Phases are rough quarters; positions are targets, not laws. This sheet structures the ROMANCE — the external plot (the bakery, the heist, the regency season) runs alongside it and may follow another sheet's logic. In a dual-POV novel both leads walk these beats, not always in step. Scenes' beat property must use these names exactly as spelled.

PHASE 1 — SETUP (0–25%)

Introduce Lead 1 — the first lead in their ordinary world, with the wound that taught them the lie they believe about love.

Introduce Lead 2 — the second lead likewise: their world, their wound, their lie. The two lies should grind against each other by design.

Meet Cute — the leads collide. Sparks — of attraction, irritation, or both — and the reader sees what neither lead will admit.

No Way — both leads resist: reasons, defenses, the lie doing its job. "Not them, not now, not ever."

Adhesion — the circumstance that forces proximity: the shared case, the fake engagement, the one room left at the inn. The reason neither can simply walk away.

PHASE 2 — FALLING IN LOVE (25–50%)

Maybe This Could Work — resistance softens into reluctant reappraisal; the other lead is not who the lie said they were.

Deepening — the fun of falling: banter, shared competence, small confidences. The promise of the premise, delivered.

Midpoint of Love — a moment of real connection — a kiss, a confession, a night — that neither can dismiss. The relationship crosses a line it cannot uncross.

Shields Up — intimacy triggers the wound; one or both leads pull back behind their defenses even as the bond keeps growing.

PHASE 3 — RETREATING FROM LOVE (50–75%)

Doubt Creeps In — the lie reasserts itself: evidence gets misread, old fears get new fuel, retreat gathers momentum.

Break Up — the dark moment: the relationship ruptures — a betrayal real or perceived, the lie spoken aloud as if it were truth.

Dark Night — each lead alone with the loss, grieving the relationship and finally facing the wound underneath the lie.

PHASE 4 — FIGHTING FOR LOVE (75–100%)

Epiphany — one lead (then the other) sees the lie AS a lie and chooses love over safety, knowing what it may cost.

Grand Gesture — the proof: a public risk, a sacrifice, a changed behavior that words alone could not deliver.

Whole-Hearted — the leads meet without shields: truths exchanged, wounds acknowledged, the new relationship chosen on honest terms.

Happily Ever After (99–100%) — the landing: happily-ever-after or happily-for-now, shown on the page — the genre's non-negotiable promise, kept.
